The director needs to arrange the scenes in a sequence where no two consecutive scenes are from the same decade. The release years of the movies are 1994, 2004, 2007, 2012, and 2015. These correspond to the decades {1990-1999}, {2000-2009}, {2000-2009}, {2010-2019}, and {2010-2019} respectively. The shooting days required are 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 days. The goal is to minimize the total number of shooting days. One optimal sequence is 1994 (4 days), 2012 (10 days), 2004 (6 days), 2015 (12 days), 2007 (8 days). This sequence ensures no two consecutive scenes are from the same decade and the total shooting days are 4 + 10 + 6 + 12 + 8 = 40 days. ******  
The director has a budget of ₹50 lakhs and the cost per day for each scene is ₹1 lakh, ₹1.5 lakhs, ₹2 lakhs, ₹2.5 lakhs, and ₹3 lakhs. The objective is to maximize the number of completed scenes without exceeding the budget. Let x1, x2, x3, x4, x5 be the number of days each scene is shot. The constraints are 1x1 + 1.5x2 + 2x3 + 2.5x4 + 3x5 ≤ 50 and x1, x2, x3, x4, x5 ≥ 0. The objective is to maximize the number of scenes completed. One optimal solution is to shoot the scenes with the lowest cost per day first. Shooting 10 days of the 4-day scene (cost ₹10 lakhs), 10 days of the 6-day scene (cost ₹15 lakhs), and 10 days of the 8-day scene (cost ₹20 lakhs) gives a total of 3 scenes completed.
